Q1: My study routine was honestly quite scattered. I would try to set aside time on weekends, maybe six hours total across the week, but with everything going on in my life at 29, it was hard to be consistent. I preferred reading and writing things down, so I would download materials when I could and work through them at my own pace. Without reliable internet at home, I had to plan ahead for when I could access things, which made spontaneous studying difficult.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the truth is deadlines drove most of my study sessions. Some weeks I would barely look at course materials, and then suddenly I would have to cram everything in before an assignment was due. It was exhausting and I knew it was not the best approach, but that is how it ended up working out.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was an upcoming assignment deadline or needing to check if there were any announcements I had missed. Sometimes I would log in just to download materials so I could study them later offline. I did not log in frequently because accessing the internet was not always easy for me, so I tried to make each login count by getting everything I needed at once.
Q4: I cannot say the LMS ever became a natural part of my routine. It was more of a tool I used when necessary rather than something I engaged with regularly. There were brief periods where I tried to check in more often, but without consistent internet access, it was hard to build that habit. I always felt a bit behind because of this limitation.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because of my learning style. I could download PDFs and text documents and study them offline, which worked well given my internet situation. I also found the discussion posts helpful when I could participate because seeing other perspectives helped me understand concepts better. The videos were good too, though I had to watch them all in one sitting when I had access.
Q6: I often ignored or delayed watching videos because they required a stable connection and a lot of time in one sitting. Some of the interactive activities I skipped because they seemed less critical than the main assignments. I know I probably missed out on some learning opportunities, but I had to prioritize what I could actually complete given my constraints. It was about survival mode, really.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or helped me understand something I was struggling with. I needed to see a clear purpose and outcome. Given how limited my study time was, I could not afford to spend hours on things that seemed optional or tangential. If something helped me complete my assignments better, it was valuable to me.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because I almost always skipped them. With only six hours a week to study and high anxiety about my performance, I focused on what was required to pass. Optional things felt like luxuries I could not afford. I sometimes felt guilty about this, but I had to make hard choices about where to put my limited energy and time.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded files and my own handwritten notes. I would print materials when I could and annotate them extensively. I also used some textbooks I had access to through the library. The LMS was really just a portal for me to get materials, not where the learning happened.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course content but the LMS would show nothing. I would spend hours reading through printed PDFs, making notes, and trying to understand concepts. Sometimes I would discuss ideas with a classmate in person. None of this showed up anywhere in the system, but it was real learning happening.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I would log in, download everything I could, and then not return to the LMS for days or even weeks. I would study those materials repeatedly offline, going through readings multiple times and writing summaries. The single download did not reflect how many times I actually engaged with that content.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became very hard. My anxiety was quite high throughout the course, and there were times when I felt overwhelmed and sad about my situation. Being 29 and struggling academically was difficult to accept. The combination of limited internet access and feeling like I was falling behind created a cycle that was hard to break.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. The fatigue was real because I was balancing multiple responsibilities outside of school. Confusion about some concepts made me avoid certain topics rather than face them. My medium stress level sometimes spiked when deadlines approached and I realized I was not as prepared as I should be. The LMS became something I dreaded rather than a helpful tool.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I would watch videos or scroll through materials but my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I was too anxious or tired to really absorb anything. I would go through the motions of studying without actually learning. These sessions felt wasted, but I did not always have the mental energy to engage more deeply.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation more than I should have. The anxiety made me avoid thinking about the exam, even though I knew it was important. I also delayed some readings that seemed particularly difficult or dense. It was a form of avoidance that I recognized was harmful, but the CBT sessions I attended helped me understand this pattern better, even if I could not always overcome it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to log in and submit work. My assignment completion rate was decent because I forced myself to meet deadlines, even if the quality suffered. Without a deadline, I would often let things slide. The structure of due dates was actually helpful for someone like me who struggled with self-motivation.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though sometimes negatively. When I received criticism, my anxiety would spike and I would sometimes avoid logging in for a while. Positive feedback motivated me to try harder on the next assignment. The problem was that feedback often came too late for me to really adjust my approach for the current course.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for someone in my situation. The expectation of regular online engagement did not match my reality of limited internet access. I appreciated having materials available for download, but the pacing assumed students could engage more frequently than I could. This mismatch contributed to my struggles.
Q19: Having downloadable text resources made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was any requirement for synchronous participation or frequent online check-ins. The discussion posts were good because I could contribute when I had access. But overall, the design assumed a level of connectivity I simply did not have consistently.
Q20: I think students might be searching for help or trying to understand what is expected without actually being able to complete the work. They might be anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Or they could be struggling with the content and spending time trying to figure things out before giving up. High activity does not mean productive activity.
Q21: This somewhat reflects my experience, though my performance was not good. My assignment scores were decent despite low LMS activity because I did most of my work offline. Someone who studies effectively with downloaded materials or external resources might not need to log in often. The LMS activity is not the whole picture of engagement.
Q22: A course log can show when someone accessed materials and what they clicked on, but it cannot show understanding or effort. It misses all offline studying, emotional struggles, and the quality of engagement. It cannot tell if someone spent two hours deeply reading a downloaded PDF or if they were just staring at a screen while anxious. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and caring about learning, not just completing tasks. It means thinking about course concepts even when you are not actively studying. For me, engagement also meant struggling through anxiety to keep trying, even when I wanted to give up. It is an internal state that external metrics cannot fully capture.
Q24: The LMS would not show my ongoing battle with anxiety or how much the CBT sessions helped me cope. It would not show the hours I spent reading printed materials or the discussions I had with classmates outside the platform. It would not capture my resilience in continuing despite failing, or the sadness I felt about my situation. My engagement was much deeper and more complicated than any log could reveal.
